edges.description: Reduced Na+/K+-ATPase function destabilizes neuronal firing in attack-generating circuits.
edges.target: Paroxysmal motor and oculomotor network instability

edges.description: Disrupted ionic homeostasis increases seizure susceptibility in a subset of patients.
Persistent circuit instability increases the chance of clinically overt epilepsy.
edges.source: Paroxysmal motor and oculomotor network instability

edges.description: Recurrent early attacks and ongoing dysfunction contribute to later persistent deficits.
edges.source: Paroxysmal motor and oculomotor network instability

edges.description: Attack-generating motor network instability produces recurrent hemiplegic episodes.
edges.source: Paroxysmal motor and oculomotor network instability

edges.description: Paroxysmal motor circuit instability produces dystonic attacks.
edges.source: Paroxysmal motor and oculomotor network instability

edges.description: Oculomotor network instability produces nystagmus and related eye-movement abnormalities.
edges.source: Paroxysmal motor and oculomotor network instability

edges.description: Brainstem and autonomic circuit instability explains autonomic spell features.
edges.source: Paroxysmal motor and oculomotor network instability

edges.description: Recurrent epilepsy and status epilepticus can worsen persistent neurologic outcomes.
Epileptogenic cortical network dysfunction produces the seizure phenotype.
Persistent early ATP1A3-related neurologic dysfunction produces developmental delay.
Chronic cerebellar and long-tract involvement produces ataxia.
Chronic motor network dysfunction produces choreoathetosis between attacks.

evidence.snippet: This work identifies de novo ATP1A3 mutations as the primary cause of AHC
Of 143 simplex occurrences, 58 had D801N (40%), 38 had E815K(26%) and 11 had G947R (8%) mutations [corrected].

nodes.description: Pathogenic ATP1A3 variants impair the neuronal alpha-3 subunit of the P-type sodium:potassium-exchanging transporter, weakening ATPase function and disrupting maintenance of transmembrane ionic gradients.

biological_processes: establishment or maintenance of transmembrane electrochemical gradient

evidence.snippet: AHC-causing mutations in this gene caused consistent reductions in ATPase activity without affecting the level of protein expression.
Mutations in ATP1A3 cause Alternating Hemiplegia of Childhood (AHC) by disrupting function of the neuronal Na+/K+ ATPase.

nodes.description: Anti-seizure medication is used when epilepsy is present, although seizures are frequently drug resistant and may require escalation.

evidence.snippet: Nineteen patients (59%) were drug resistant.

nodes.description: Ataxia is a common chronic motor manifestation that can persist between attacks.

evidence.snippet: pervasive neurological disabilities (e.g., developmental delay, learning disabilities, choreoathetosis, and ataxia).

nodes.description: Respiratory and broader autonomic dysregulation are part of the classic paroxysmal AHC presentation.

evidence.snippet: Alternating Hemiplegia of Childhood (AHC) is a rare disorder with onset in the first 18 months of life characterized by stereotyped paroxysmal manifestations of tonic and dystonic attacks, nystagmus with other oculomotor abnormalities, respiratory and autonomic dysfunctions.

nodes.description: Choreoathetosis can persist between attacks as part of the chronic movement disorder phenotype.

evidence.snippet: pervasive neurological disabilities (e.g., developmental delay, learning disabilities, choreoathetosis, and ataxia).

nodes.description: Recurrent attacks, epilepsy, and ATP1A3 dysfunction converge on persistent cognitive, motor, and behavioral disability.

evidence.snippet: Patients with an E815K mutation demonstrate an earlier age of onset, more severe motor impairment and a higher prevalence of status epilepticus.
All patients had cognitive impairment.
Some disorders caused by ATP1A3 variants have been defined as ATP1A3-related disorders

nodes.description: Paroxysmal dystonic posturing and tonic attacks are typical, sometimes accompanying hemiplegic spells.

evidence.snippet: Alternating Hemiplegia of Childhood (AHC) is a rare disorder with onset in the first 18 months of life characterized by stereotyped paroxysmal manifestations of tonic and dystonic attacks, nystagmus with other oculomotor abnormalities, respiratory and autonomic dysfunctions.

nodes.description: AHC can produce focal or generalized seizures, status epilepticus, and nonepileptic reduced-awareness spells that require EEG-based distinction.

evidence.snippet: Thirty-two of 51 patients had epilepsy: 18 focal seizures, frontal more frequently than temporal, and then posterior.
EEG may become epileptiform only on follow-up.

nodes.description: Recurrent transient hemiplegic attacks are the defining feature of AHC and may alternate sides or become bilateral.

evidence.snippet: Alternating hemiplegia of childhood (AHC) is characterized by recurrent episodes of hemiplegia which may alternate sides between attacks.

nodes.description: Delayed motor, language, and cognitive development are common and may be persistent, especially in more severe ATP1A3-related disease.

evidence.snippet: AHC is often associated with epileptic seizures and developmental delay.

nodes.description: Abnormal eye movements, particularly nystagmus, are common early clinical clues and may precede hemiplegic attacks.

evidence.snippet: the common earliest manifestations are dystonic or tonic attacks and nystagmus.

nodes.description: ATP1A3 pump failure produces episodic instability in motor and oculomotor circuits, yielding hemiplegic attacks, dystonia, nystagmus, and autonomic spell clustering.
nodes.id: Paroxysmal motor and oculomotor network instability

evidence.snippet: Alternating Hemiplegia of Childhood (AHC) is a rare disorder with onset in the first 18 months of life characterized by stereotyped paroxysmal manifestations of tonic and dystonic attacks, nystagmus with other oculomotor abnormalities, respiratory and autonomic dysfunctions.
The variability of individual clinical presentations and evolution of symptoms have made diagnosis difficult.

nodes.description: Epilepsy is common in AHC, can emerge early or later in the disease course, and is often drug resistant.

evidence.snippet: Thirty-two of 51 patients had epilepsy: 18 focal seizures, frontal more frequently than temporal, and then posterior.
